passages.explanation: Lazarus, the brother of Mary and Martha, is seriously ill in Bethany. John identifies Mary as the woman who later anoints Jesus with perfume. The sisters send word to Jesus, framing it personally: the one you love is sick. They don't ask for anything specific — they trust Jesus will understand what they need.

Jesus says the sickness won't end in death — it's for God's glory and will result in the Son being glorified. John emphasises that Jesus loved this family. But instead of rushing to Bethany, he deliberately stays where he is for two more days. Then he tells his disciples they're going back to Judea.

The disciples are alarmed — the last time they were in Judea, people tried to stone Jesus. He responds with a metaphor about walking in daylight versus darkness: he has work to do while there's still time. He tells them Lazarus has 'fallen asleep' and he's going to wake him. The disciples take this literally — if he's sleeping, he'll recover. Jesus says plainly: Lazarus is dead. Then he says something unexpected — he's glad he wasn't there, so that the disciples can believe through what's about to happen. Thomas, called Didymus, responds with grim loyalty: let's go too, so we can die with him.

Jesus arrives to find Lazarus has been in the tomb four days — well past the point where anyone could question whether he was really dead. Bethany is close to Jerusalem, so many Jews have come to console Martha and Mary. Martha goes out to meet Jesus and says what she's clearly been thinking: if you had been here, my brother wouldn't have died. But she adds that she still believes God will give Jesus whatever he asks.

Jesus tells Martha her brother will rise again. She assumes he means in the final resurrection at the end of time. Jesus redirects her: 'I am the resurrection and the life.' Anyone who believes in him will live even if they die, and anyone who lives and believes will never truly die. He asks if she believes this. Martha affirms it — she believes he is the Christ, the Son of God, the one who was promised to come into the world.
Martha goes to get Mary, telling her quietly that the Teacher is asking for her. Mary gets up quickly. The mourners assume she's going to the tomb to weep and follow her.

Mary reaches Jesus and falls at his feet, saying the same thing Martha said: if you had been here, he wouldn't have died. When Jesus sees her weeping and the crowd weeping with her, he is deeply moved — the text says he groaned in his spirit and was troubled. He asks where they've laid Lazarus. They say come and see. Then the shortest verse in most English Bibles: Jesus wept. Some of the onlookers see this as proof of his love for Lazarus. Others question why the man who healed the blind couldn't have prevented this death.

Jesus, still deeply moved, comes to the tomb — a cave with a stone across the entrance. He tells them to remove the stone. Martha objects: Lazarus has been dead four days and there will be a smell. Jesus reminds her: if you believe, you'll see God's glory. They remove the stone. Jesus prays aloud — not for his own benefit but so the crowd will know the Father sent him. Then he calls out in a loud voice: 'Lazarus, come out.' Lazarus walks out, still wrapped in burial cloths. Jesus tells them to unwrap him and let him go.

Many of the Jews who witnessed this believe in Jesus. But some go to the Pharisees and report what happened. The chief priests and Pharisees convene a council. Their concern is practical, not theological: Jesus is performing too many signs, everyone will believe in him, and the Romans will see the movement as a political threat and destroy the temple and the nation.

Caiaphas, the high priest, cuts through the debate: it's better for one man to die than for the whole nation to be destroyed. John notes that Caiaphas didn't realise the full meaning of his own words — he was unwittingly prophesying that Jesus would die for the nation, and not only the nation but to gather all of God's scattered children together. From this point, the authorities actively plot to kill Jesus. Jesus withdraws to a town called Ephraim near the wilderness and stays there with his disciples.

Passover is approaching. People arriving in Jerusalem are looking for Jesus, wondering whether he'll come to the feast. The chief priests and Pharisees have issued an order: anyone who knows where Jesus is must report it so they can arrest him.
